main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Recombination Flow Matching (RFM) model: a generative model that recombines protein segments, preserves their structural integrity, and optimizes their spatial arrangement using principles from biological evolution, particularly recombination"],"problems":["Generating novel protein structures that are distinct from those found in nature","Current protein design methods fail to fully leverage biological recombination and struggle to generate truly novel protein structures","Existing generative models overlook the significance of larger protein segments and have difficulty preserving segment integrity and optimizing their arrangement"],"datasets":["Benchmark datasets of protein structures (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Structural novelty of generated proteins","Performance compared to existing methods (likely using metrics such as novelty, diversity, and possibly structural similarity, though not explicitly named in the excerpt)"],"results":[{"metric":"Structural novelty (relative to existing methods)","value":"RFM significantly outperforms established methods in producing structurally novel proteins"}],"novelty_claims":["RFM is the first model to incorporate recombination into protein design for enhancing the novelty of protein structures","RFM preserves the structural integrity of recombined protein segments and automates the optimization of their spatial arrangement","RFM can generalize from recombining two proteins to multiple proteins, indicating it learns underlying principles of recombination"]}

methods: Recombination Flow Matching (RFM) model: a generative model that recombines protein segments, preserves their structural integrity, and optimizes their spatial arrangement using principles from biological evolution, particularly recombination
problems: Generating novel protein structures that are distinct from those found in nature
Current protein design methods fail to fully leverage biological recombination and struggle to generate truly novel protein structures
Existing generative models overlook the significance of larger protein segments and have difficulty preserving segment integrity and optimizing their arrangement
datasets: Benchmark datasets of protein structures (specific names not provided in the excerpt)
metrics: Performance compared to existing methods (likely using metrics such as novelty, diversity, and possibly structural similarity, though not explicitly named in the excerpt)

results.value: RFM significantly outperforms established methods in producing structurally novel proteins

novelty_claims: RFM is the first model to incorporate recombination into protein design for enhancing the novelty of protein structures
RFM preserves the structural integrity of recombined protein segments and automates the optimization of their spatial arrangement
RFM can generalize from recombining two proteins to multiple proteins, indicating it learns underlying principles of recombination

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SE(3) invariant diffusion model on SE(3)^N for protein backbones","FrameDiff: a novel framework for learning SE(3) equivariant score over multiple frames","Theoretical construction of diffusion process on SE(3)^N, separating translations and rotations","Denoising score matching (DSM) training on Brownian motion on compact Lie groups"],"problems":["Lack of principled methodological framework for diffusion on SE(3) for protein backbone generation","Generating novel, designable protein backbones that generalize beyond known structures","Reducing reliance on pretrained protein structure prediction networks in protein design","Bridging the gap between deep learning methods and theoretical foundations for SE(3) diffusion"],"datasets":["Not explicitly specified in the provided content (likely protein structure datasets for monomer backbone generation)"],"metrics":["In-silico designability success rate","Diversity of generated protein monomers","Novelty of generated protein structures"],"results":[{"metric":"In-silico designability success rate","value":"Second only to RFdiffusion (a pretrained model with 4x more parameters)"}],"novelty_claims":["First principled theoretical and methodological framework for SE(3) invariant diffusion models on multiple frames (SE(3)^N) for protein backbone generation","FrameDiff does not require pretraining on protein structure prediction networks, unlike previous methods","FrameDiff can generate designable, diverse, and novel protein monomers up to 500 amino acids","Theoretical characterization of Brownian motion on compact Lie groups (SO(3)) for denoising score matching training","Separation of translation and rotation in the forward diffusion process on SE(3)^N"]}

methods: SE(3) invariant diffusion model on SE(3)^N for protein backbones
FrameDiff: a novel framework for learning SE(3) equivariant score over multiple frames
Theoretical construction of diffusion process on SE(3)^N, separating translations and rotations
Denoising score matching (DSM) training on Brownian motion on compact Lie groups
problems: Lack of principled methodological framework for diffusion on SE(3) for protein backbone generation
Generating novel, designable protein backbones that generalize beyond known structures
Reducing reliance on pretrained protein structure prediction networks in protein design
Bridging the gap between deep learning methods and theoretical foundations for SE(3) diffusion
datasets: Not explicitly specified in the provided content (likely protein structure datasets for monomer backbone generation)

results.value: Second only to RFdiffusion (a pretrained model with 4x more parameters)

novelty_claims: First principled theoretical and methodological framework for SE(3) invariant diffusion models on multiple frames (SE(3)^N) for protein backbone generation
FrameDiff does not require pretraining on protein structure prediction networks, unlike previous methods
FrameDiff can generate designable, diverse, and novel protein monomers up to 500 amino acids

Separation of translation and rotation in the forward diffusion process on SE(3)^N

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Flow-based generative modeling approach for protein conformational landscapes","Repurposing AlphaFold and ESMFold under a custom flow matching framework (AlphaFlow and ESMFlow)","Fine-tuning on PDB structures and further training on molecular dynamics (MD) ensembles (ATLAS dataset)","Custom flow matching framework with scale-invariant noising process"],"problems":["Generating protein structural ensembles that capture conformational heterogeneity","Overcoming limitations of inference-time MSA ablation in ensemble generation","Generalizing ensemble generation to PLM-based structure predictors (e.g., ESMFold)","Learning from and sampling protein ensembles beyond the PDB, including MD simulations"],"datasets":["PDB (Protein Data Bank)","ATLAS dataset of molecular dynamics simulations"],"metrics":["Precision","Diversity","Conformational flexibility","Distributional modeling of atomic positions","Higher-order ensemble observables (e.g., intermittent contacts, solvent exposure)","Wall-clock convergence to equilibrium properties"],"results":[{"metric":"Precision-diversity Pareto frontier","value":"AlphaFlow and ESMFlow substantially surpass MSA ablation baselines on test set of conformationally heterogeneous proteins"},{"metric":"Prediction of conformational flexibility and ensemble observables","value":"AlphaFlow substantially surpasses MSA baselines on test proteins structurally dissimilar from training set"},{"metric":"Wall-clock convergence to equilibrium properties","value":"AlphaFlow achieves faster convergence than replicate MD trajectories when diversifying a static PDB structure"}],"novelty_claims":["First application of flow matching to repurpose noising process resilient to missing/cropped residues","Ability to train and sample from arbitrary protein ensembles, including those from MD simulations, not just PDB","Superior combination of precision and diversity compared to MSA ablation baselines","Faster convergence to equilibrium properties than MD simulations, enabling efficient ensemble generation as a proxy for expensive methods: Flow-based generative modeling approach for protein conformational landscapes
Repurposing AlphaFold and ESMFold under a custom flow matching framework (AlphaFlow and ESMFlow)
Fine-tuning on PDB structures and further training on molecular dynamics (MD) ensembles (ATLAS dataset)
Custom flow matching framework with scale-invariant noising process
problems: Generating protein structural ensembles that capture conformational heterogeneity
Overcoming limitations of inference-time MSA ablation in ensemble generation
Generalizing ensemble generation to PLM-based structure predictors (e.g., ESMFold)
Learning from and sampling protein ensembles beyond the PDB, including MD simulations
datasets: PDB (Protein Data Bank)
ATLAS dataset of molecular dynamics simulations
metrics: Higher-order ensemble observables (e.g., intermittent contacts, solvent exposure)

results.value: AlphaFlow and ESMFlow substantially surpass MSA ablation baselines on test set of conformationally heterogeneous proteins

results.metric: Prediction of conformational flexibility and ensemble observables
results.value: AlphaFlow substantially surpasses MSA baselines on test proteins structurally dissimilar from training set
AlphaFlow achieves faster convergence than replicate MD trajectories when diversifying a static PDB structure

novelty_claims: First application of flow matching to repurpose AlphaFold and ESMFold as sequence-conditioned generative models for protein structure ensembles
Custom flow matching framework tailored to protein structure prediction, including a scale-invariant noising process resilient to missing/cropped residues
Ability to train and sample from arbitrary protein ensembles, including those from MD simulations, not just PDB
Superior combination of precision and diversity compared to MSA ablation baselines
Faster convergence to equilibrium properties than MD simulations, enabling efficient ensemble generation as a proxy for expensive physics-based simulations

novelty_claims: General protein language models can efficiently guide the evolution of human antibodies with no information about the target antigen, binding specificity, or protein structure.
The approach requires screening only 20 or fewer variants per antibody across two rounds of laboratory evolution.
The same language models can guide efficient evolution across diverse protein families and selection pressures, generalizing beyond antibodies.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FoldFlow-Base: a simulation-free continuous normalizing flow (CNF) for SE(3)^N-invariant distributions with a closed-form conditional vector field for SO(3)","FoldFlow-OT: an accelerated version of FoldFlow-Base using Riemannian Optimal Transport (OT) to construct simpler and shorter flows via a Monge map on SE(3)^N","FoldFlow-SFM: a simulation-free model that learns stochastic continuous-time dynamics (stochastic bridge) on SE(3)^N, combining Riemannian OT and simulation-free training"],"problems":["Generative modeling of protein backbones as distributions over SE(3)^N","Efficient and stable training of generative models for protein structure generation without expensive SDE simulation","Generation of novel, diverse, and designable protein structures for de novo protein design","Equilibrium conformation generation for molecular dynamics"],"datasets":["Protein backbone datasets (up to 300 amino acids) for training and evaluation (exact dataset names not specified in the provided text)"],"metrics":["In-silico designability","Novelty (of generated proteins)","Training speed and stability","Quality of equilibrium conformation generation"],"results":[{"metric":"In-silico designability (FoldFlow-OT vs FrameDiff)","value":"FoldFlow-OT outperforms FrameDiff (SOTA non-pretrained diffusion model)"},{"metric":"Novelty (FoldFlow-SFM vs RFDiffusion)","value":"FoldFlow-SFM is competitive with RFDiffusion (gold-standard) using less compute and data"}],"novelty_claims":["First family of continuous normalizing flows (CNFs) tailored for SE(3)^N-invariant distributions for protein backbone generation","Simulation-free training for both deterministic and stochastic flows on SE(3)^N, avoiding expensive SDE simulation","Closed-form expression for the ground truth conditional vector field for SO(3) in FoldFlow-Base, increasing speed and stability","Use of Riemannian Optimal Transport (OT) to accelerate training and construct simpler flows via a Monge map on SE(3)^N","Ability to start from informative priors, enabling new applications such as equilibrium conformation generation","FoldFlow models are more stable and faster to train than diffusion-based approaches and can map any invariant source to any invariant target distribution over SE(3)"]}

methods: FoldFlow-Base: a simulation-free continuous normalizing flow (CNF) for SE(3)^N-invariant distributions with a closed-form conditional vector field for SO(3)
FoldFlow-OT: an accelerated version of FoldFlow-Base using Riemannian Optimal Transport (OT) to construct simpler and shorter flows via a Monge map on SE(3)^N
FoldFlow-SFM: a simulation-free model that learns stochastic continuous-time dynamics (stochastic bridge) on SE(3)^N, combining Riemannian OT and simulation-free training
problems: Generative modeling of protein backbones as distributions over SE(3)^N
Efficient and stable training of generative models for protein structure generation without expensive SDE simulation
Generation of novel, diverse, and designable protein structures for de novo protein design
Equilibrium conformation generation for molecular dynamics
datasets: Protein backbone datasets (up to 300 amino acids) for training and evaluation (exact dataset names not specified in the provided text)

results.value: FoldFlow-OT outperforms FrameDiff (SOTA non-pretrained diffusion model)
FoldFlow-SFM is competitive with RFDiffusion (gold-standard) using less compute and data

novelty_claims: First family of continuous normalizing flows (CNFs) tailored for SE(3)^N-invariant distributions for protein backbone generation
Simulation-free training for both deterministic and stochastic flows on SE(3)^N, avoiding expensive SDE simulation
Closed-form expression for the ground truth conditional vector field for SO(3) in FoldFlow-Base, increasing speed and stability
Use of Riemannian Optimal Transport (OT) to accelerate training and construct simpler flows via a Monge map on SE(3)^N
Ability to start from informative priors, enabling new applications such as equilibrium conformation generation
FoldFlow models are more stable and faster to train than diffusion-based approaches and can map any invariant source to any invariant target distribution over SE(3)

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["3D generative model for molecule generation conditioned on 3D protein binding sites","Neural network architecture modeling atom occurrence probability in 3D space","Rotationally invariant graph neural networks for context perception","Auto-regressive sampling algorithm for sequential atom placement"],"problems":["Generating molecules that bind to specific protein binding sites (structure-based drug design)","Incorporating 3D spatial information into molecular generative models","Generating valid and diverse molecules for specific 3D protein targets","Overcoming limitations of string-based and graph-based generative models lacking spatial awareness"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Binding affinity to specific targets","Drug-likeness score (QED)","Synthetic accessibility score (SA)"],"results":[{"metric":"Binding affinity","value":"Molecules generated exhibit high binding affinity to specific targets"},{"metric":"QED (drug-likeness score)","value":"Generated molecules have fairly high QED scores"},{"metric":"SA (synthetic accessibility score)","value":"Generated molecules have fairly high SA scores"}],"novelty_claims":["First 3D generative model that generates molecules conditioned on a designated 3D protein binding site by modeling atom occurrence probability in 3D space","Proposes an auto-regressive sampling scheme for sequential atom placement, enabling valid and diverse molecule generation without post-processing or additional latent variables","Utilizes rotationally invariant graph neural networks to ensure equivariance to rotation and translation of the protein binding site context","Demonstrates applicability to both molecule generation and linker design tasks"]}

methods: 3D generative model for molecule generation conditioned on 3D protein binding sites
Neural network architecture modeling atom occurrence probability in 3D space
Rotationally invariant graph neural networks for context perception
Auto-regressive sampling algorithm for sequential atom placement
problems: Generating molecules that bind to specific protein binding sites (structure-based drug design)
Incorporating 3D spatial information into molecular generative models
Generating valid and diverse molecules for specific 3D protein targets
Overcoming limitations of string-based and graph-based generative models lacking spatial awareness

results.value: Molecules generated exhibit high binding affinity to specific targets
Generated molecules have fairly high QED scores
Generated molecules have fairly high SA scores

novelty_claims: First 3D generative model that generates molecules conditioned on a designated 3D protein binding site by modeling atom occurrence probability in 3D space
Proposes an auto-regressive sampling scheme for sequential atom placement, enabling valid and diverse molecule generation without post-processing or additional latent variables
Utilizes rotationally invariant graph neural networks to ensure equivariance to rotation and translation of the protein binding site context
Demonstrates applicability to both molecule generation and linker design tasks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Vector Field Network (VFN): a structure encoder using vector field operators to extract geometric feature vectors between frame-anchored virtual atoms","VFN-Diff: VFN-based model for protein diffusion (generative modeling)","VFN-IF: VFN-based model for protein inverse folding","VFN-IFE: VFN-IF variant equipped with an external knowledge base (e.g., ESM model)"],"problems":["Atom representation bottleneck in protein structure encoders for de novo protein design, especially when only residue backbone frames are available and atom-level features are absent","Limited expressiveness and generality of prior encoders (e.g., IPA) for modeling protein residue frames in diffusion and inverse folding tasks"],"datasets":["PDB (Protein Data Bank) database"],"metrics":["Designability","Diversity","Sequence recovery rate","Precision"],"results":[{"metric":"Designability (protein diffusion, VFN-Diff vs. IPA)","value":"67.04% vs. 53.58%"},{"metric":"Diversity (protein diffusion, VFN-Diff vs. IPA)","value":"66.54% vs. 51.98%"},{"metric":"Sequence recovery rate (inverse folding, VFN-IF vs. PiFold)","value":"54.74% vs. 51.66%"},{"metric":"Sequence recovery rate (inverse folding, VFN-IF trained on full PDB)","value":"57.14%"},{"metric":"Precision (inverse folding, VFN-IFE vs. LM-Design)","value":"62.64% vs. 55.65%"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of the Vector Field Network (VFN), a novel structure encoder that uses vector field operators to extract multiple geometric feature vectors between frame-anchored virtual atoms, overcoming the atom representation bottleneck in protein design tasks","VFN can flexibly model both frames and atoms, allowing it to serve as a universal encoder for protein structure tasks","VFN achieves state-of-the-art performance in both protein diffusion (generative modeling) and inverse folding tasks, significantly outperforming previous methods such as IPA, PiFold, and LM-Design","Proposal of VFN-IFE, a VFN-based model equipped with an external knowledge base, achieving substantial improvements in precision over previous ESM-based methods"]}

methods: Vector Field Network (VFN): a structure encoder using vector field operators to extract geometric feature vectors between frame-anchored virtual atoms
VFN-Diff: VFN-based model for protein diffusion (generative modeling)
VFN-IF: VFN-based model for protein inverse folding
VFN-IFE: VFN-IF variant equipped with an external knowledge base (e.g., ESM model)
problems: Atom representation bottleneck in protein structure encoders for de novo protein design, especially when only residue backbone frames are available and atom-level features are absent
Limited expressiveness and generality of prior encoders (e.g., IPA) for modeling protein residue frames in diffusion and inverse folding tasks
datasets: PDB (Protein Data Bank) database

results.metric: Sequence recovery rate (inverse folding, VFN-IF vs. PiFold)
Sequence recovery rate (inverse folding, VFN-IF trained on full PDB)

novelty_claims: Introduction of the Vector Field Network (VFN), a novel structure encoder that uses vector field operators to extract multiple geometric feature vectors between frame-anchored virtual atoms, overcoming the atom representation bottleneck in protein design tasks
VFN can flexibly model both frames and atoms, allowing it to serve as a universal encoder for protein structure tasks
VFN achieves state-of-the-art performance in both protein diffusion (generative modeling) and inverse folding tasks, significantly outperforming previous methods such as IPA, PiFold, and LM-Design
Proposal of VFN-IFE, a VFN-based model equipped with an external knowledge base, achieving substantial improvements in precision over previous ESM-based methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Vertical comparison of modern and resurrected ancestral proteins","Dynamic Flexibility Index (DFI) to quantify site-specific flexibility","Dynamic Coupling Index (DCI) to quantify site-specific dynamic coupling"],"problems":["Understanding the fundamental principles behind protein evolution","Investigating how conformational dynamics and allostery modulate protein evolution","Explaining the mechanisms by which proteins adapt new functions in response to environmental changes","Understanding the impact of disease-associated mutations on protein dynamics"],"datasets":["Sequences of modern proteins","Sequences of resurrected ancestral proteins","Human exome mutation data"],"metrics":["Dynamic Flexibility Index (DFI)","Dynamic Coupling Index (DCI)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Provides a vertical comparison of modern and ancestral proteins focusing on dynamical properties, rather than traditional horizontal (comparative) studies.","Demonstrates that migration of functionally critical rigid sites (hinges) is a mechanism for rapid protein evolution.","Shows that disease-associated mutations can alter flexibility at distal sites, especially affecting active site dynamics."]}

novelty_claims: Provides a vertical comparison of modern and ancestral proteins focusing on dynamical properties, rather than traditional horizontal (comparative) studies.

Shows that disease-associated mutations can alter flexibility at distal sites, especially affecting active site dynamics.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Ancestral Sequence Reconstruction (ASR)","Computational phylogenetic analysis","DNA sequencing and synthesis"],"problems":["Understanding the evolution of genes and proteins in response to changing environments","Inferring evolutionary precursors of extant proteins in the absence of fossil DNA","Designing novel proteins with desirable properties using ancestral sequences","Accurately inferring ancestral states despite uncertainty in evolutionary models, incomplete sequences, and limited phylogenetic trees"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["ASR is presented not only as a tool for studying protein evolution but also as a practical approach for protein engineering, enabling the design of novel proteins with desirable properties such as thermostability and broad substrate range.","The paper highlights the recent escalation in the number of ancient proteins resurrected due to advances in DNA sequencing, synthesis, and computational phylogenetics, facilitating the use of ASR in synthetic biology."]}

Computational phylogenetic analysis
DNA sequencing and synthesis
problems: Understanding the evolution of genes and proteins in response to changing environments
Inferring evolutionary precursors of extant proteins in the absence of fossil DNA

novelty_claims: ASR is presented not only as a tool for studying protein evolution but also as a practical approach for protein engineering, enabling the design of novel proteins with desirable properties such as thermostability and broad substrate range.

The paper highlights the recent escalation in the number of ancient proteins resurrected due to advances in DNA sequencing, synthesis, and computational phylogenetics, facilitating the use of ASR in synthetic biology.

results.value: ~100% across a 5 nt editable window
Expanded to 8 nt by extending sgRNA length

novelty_claims: Development of a highly efficient base editor for in situ protein evolution in vivo in Bacillus subtilis
Base editor achieves ~100% cytidine-to-thymidine mutation efficiency across a 5 nt window, higher than other base editors
Editable window can be expanded to 8 nt by modifying sgRNA
Conversion efficiency can be regulated by culture conditions
